data.title: Eneos in final talks for $2B in Asian assets from Chevron - Bloomberg
data.description:
Japan's Eneos Holdings (JXHLY) is the last remaining bidder for some of Chevron's (CVX) Asian assets in a deal that might be valued at more than $2B, with the companies aiming to reach an agreement as soon as this quarter, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

The assets reportedly up for sale include stakes in refineries and gas stations in Singapore, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Australia.

Eneos (JXHLY) was ahead of rival bidders including Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and Vitol for a 50% stake in Chevron’s (CVX) 290K bbl/day Singapore oil refinery, according to previous reports.

The refinery joint venture with PetroChina, which relies on the Middle East for the bulk of ​its crude imports, is operating at a lower rate of ​60% ⁠after the Middle East war drastically reduced supply to the plant, Reuters reported previously.

Separately, Libya's National Oil Corporation said it signed ‌a memorandum of understanding with Chevron (CVX) to assess shale ​oil and gas reserves in some of the ​country's sedimentary ​basins.

Preliminary estimates ​showed ​~⁠123 Tcf ​of natural gas and ​~⁠18B barrels of oil ⁠in ​the ​Libyan basins, Libya's NOC said.

data.title: Itron anticipates Q2 revenue of $560M-$570M with non-GAAP EPS of $1.25-$1.35 as backlog holds at $4.4B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Itron, Inc. (ITRI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Itron had a solid start to the year. Our first quarter results were ahead of expectations due to strong execution from our teams and some first half projects progressing ahead of schedule." (CEO, President & Director Thomas Deitrich)
  • "Revenue of $587 million, adjusted EBITDA of $92 million, non-GAAP earnings per share of $1.49 and free cash flow of $79 million." (CEO Deitrich)
  • "Our first quarter bookings were $476 million, bringing the total backlog to $4.4 billion at quarter end, in line with our expectations." (CEO Deitrich)
  • "Outcomes and Resiliency Solutions combined now represent 25% of total backlog and that share is growing." (CEO Deitrich)
  • "First quarter revenue of $587 million was above our outlook range due to an acceleration of certain first half project deployments." (Senior VP & CFO Joan Hooper)
  • "We anticipate Q2 revenue to be within a range of $560 million to $570 million" and "second quarter non-GAAP EPS to be within the range of $1.25 to $1.35 per diluted share." (CFO Hooper)

Outlook

  • "We anticipate the first half to be consistent with our initial guidance." (CEO Deitrich)
  • "Our current view of the first half of 2026 is consistent with our thinking when we set the annual outlook back in February." (CFO Hooper)
  • "What would drive an uptick in the second half of the year is project deployments primarily in networks." (CFO Hooper)
  • "I would say that we expected the year to be back half loaded. That was part of the initial guidance." (CEO Deitrich)

Financial Results

  • "Gross margin was 450 basis points higher than last year due to favorable mix and operational efficiencies." (CFO Hooper)
  • "GAAP net income of $53 million or $1.18 per diluted share compares to $65 million or $1.42 in the prior year." (CFO Hooper)
  • "Adjusted gross margin of 40.7%" and "non-GAAP net income for the quarter was $68 million or $1.49 per diluted share." (CFO Hooper)
  • "Outcomes revenue increased 20% on a constant currency basis" and "Resiliency Solutions... contributed $16 million of revenue in Q1." (CFO Hooper)
  • "Total debt was $1.61 billion, and cash and equivalents were $713 million" and "As of March 31, net leverage was 2.4x." (CFO Hooper)

Q&A

  • Noah Kaye, Oppenheimer: asked what drove Q1 project acceleration, the Q2 step-down, and what supports a 2H pickup; CFO Hooper said "It was primarily in the network business, but also a little bit in devices" and CEO Deitrich added "no constraints when it comes to supply chain. So material was fine, labor was fine."
  • Noah Kaye, Oppenheimer: asked about customer behavior and DOE funding (SPARK); CEO Deitrich said "water in Europe continues to be strong," "gas in North America is particularly strong," and "In general, we have not seen any cancellations... Customers need to do these things. They weren't discretionary."
  • Ben Kallo, Baird: asked whether Analyst Day targets changed and how recurring backlog maps to Outcomes/Resiliency; CEO Deitrich said "Still, we are very much ahead of those '27 targets" and added "Our Outcomes segment generally runs somewhere between 2/3 to 3.75 recurring revenue" while "The vast majority of it is recurring revenue" for Resiliency Solutions.
  • Ben Kallo, Baird: asked about capital allocation and M&A; CFO Hooper said "our first priority in '26 is the successful integration of Urbint and Locusview" and "we're not actively going to seek something to buy in '26."
  • Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice: asked about acquisition synergies and book-to-bill; CFO Hooper said "the results to date have not really been any synergies per se" and CEO Deitrich said "Bookings in the networking space are inherently a bit lumpy."
  • Scott Graham, Seaport: pressed on confidence in the 2H network deployment uptick and backlog-to-PO conversion risk; CEO Deitrich said "nothing has changed in the marketplace at all" and "there's backlog there. It just needs to be converted."
  • Robert Zolper, Raymond James: asked RPO vs. backlog and gross margin drivers; CFO Hooper said RPO "backs off contracts that have termination for convenience clause" and emphasized "we use our full backlog"; CEO Deitrich said "the last of some of that pre-inflation run-up backlog" has "now fully played through."
  • Joseph Osha, Guggenheim: asked when Resiliency reaches corporate-level operating margins; CFO Hooper reiterated that Resiliency is "immediately accretive to Itron's revenue growth, gross margins and EBITDA" and "by the time we're into '27 completely accretive on an EPS level."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on timing/visibility: questions repeatedly challenged whether the 2H ramp is supported by "TTM book-to-bill being below 0.9" (Scott Graham, Seaport) and sought clarity on "book-to-bill and when that might trend back over one" (Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice).
  • Slightly positive management tone with reassurance on execution and funding stability: management repeatedly leaned on firmness such as "nothing has changed in the marketplace at all" (CEO Deitrich) and "we have not seen any cancellations" (CEO Deitrich), while acknowledging volatility: "The operating environment remains volatile domestically and globally, and that volatility creates risks." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Versus last quarter, management language stayed confident on multi-year demand, but Q1 discussion shifted toward near-term phasing (Q1 acceleration/Q2 step-down) rather than Q4’s emphasis on "record earnings and profitability." (CEO Deitrich, prior quarter)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized delivery timing effects: "Q1 benefited from an acceleration of first half projects" (CFO Hooper), while Q4 emphasized "record earnings and profitability" and "non-GAAP earnings per share of $2.46." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Resiliency Solutions contribution broadened: Q4 reported Resiliency Solutions revenue of $3 million (CFO Hooper, prior quarter) versus Q1 reporting $16 million in revenue and "The integration... is on track." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Backlog and bookings moderated from Q4 levels: Q4 cited bookings of $737 million and backlog of $4.5 billion (CEO Deitrich, prior quarter) versus Q1 bookings of $476 million and backlog of $4.4 billion (CEO Deitrich), with management reiterating that networking bookings are "lumpy." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Analyst focus shifted from understanding new ARR framing and adoption metrics in Q4 to testing the durability of the 2H deployment ramp and conversion mechanics in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our customers continue to work in a complex environment, balancing global uncertainty, affordability concerns, resiliency imperatives and growing demand variability." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Timing and conversion risk remained central: "there's backlog there. It just needs to be converted" based on "timing of deployments." (CEO Deitrich)
  • Integration and synergy timing for acquisitions: CFO Hooper said "we haven't spent a lot of time trying to build synergies" and prioritized integration "plumbing" before driving cross-sell. (CFO Hooper)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an execution-driven start with project timing pulling network deployments forward, while reiterating that the first-half setup remains consistent with February’s annual view and that the year is expected to be back-half weighted on network deployments. The call highlighted a $4.4B backlog, rising mix of Outcomes and Resiliency Solutions in backlog, and continued emphasis on recurring revenue growth, margin discipline, and acquisition integration, while analysts repeatedly tested the visibility of the second-half ramp and the mechanics of converting backlog into purchase orders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street trades lower as OpenAI misses targets and FOMC rate decision lurks
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages traded lower on Tuesday, with the markets under some pressure as a report on OpenAI missing its own targets has spooked investors.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.7%, while the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was near flat%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, six of the 11 S&P segments are in the green, with energy at the top. Going the other way, info tech has been the weakest performer. 

U.S. Treasury yields moved higher. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose to 3.83%, while the 10-year yield (US10Y) climbed to 4.35% and the 30-year yield (US30Y) advanced to 4.95%, reflecting upward pressure as investors assessed the macro backdrop. 

OpenAI (OPENAI) recently missed its own targets for new users and revenue, stumbles that have raised concern among some company leaders about whether it will be able to support its massive spending on data centers. 

On the earnings front, General Motors (GM) posted solid quarterly results and beat Wall Street’s profit expectations by nearly 30%. Coca-Cola (KO) topped estimates with its first-quarter earnings report. 

Geopolitical tensions remained in focus after Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi arrived in Russia, where he is expected to meet President Vladimir Putin, even as negotiations with the U.S. reached a stalemate over the weekend.

data.title: Hillman outlines $1.63B-$1.73B 2026 sales outlook while targeting $2.5B net sales by 2030
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hillman Solutions Corp. (HLMN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Over the next 5 years, we are confident we will grow Hillman’s total net sales to $2.5 billion in 2030,” said Jon Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director), adding, “To reach this number, we are targeting 8% to 12% growth per year.”
  • “Subsequent to the end of the quarter, we closed on 2 acquisitions. Campbell Chain & Fittings and Delaney Hardware,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director). He said, “We anticipate that Campbell will contribute over $20 million of net sales and Delaney will contribute over $10 million of net sales to Hillman this year,” and “we are raising our full year net sales guidance range by the same amount.”
  • “Net sales in the first quarter of 2026 totaled $370.1 million, which was an increase of 3% versus the first quarter of 2025,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director). He added, “For the quarter, adjusted EBITDA decreased 8% to $50.1 million compared to $54.5 million during the year ago quarter.”
  • “PS had a tough quarter, down 17% in total,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director), and “we expect to see PS improve throughout the year, but it is expected to remain below 2025 levels for the full year.”
  • “Robotics and Digital Solutions, or RDS, had a great quarter,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director). He said, “Net sales were up 6% versus the year ago quarter and adjusted EBITDA increased by 11.4% to $16.2 million,” and “we have approximately 3,900 MinuteKey 3.5 machines in the field.”
  • “First quarter adjusted gross margin decreased by 130 basis points to 45.6%,” said Robert Kraft (CFO & Treasurer), and “net cash used for operating activities was $19.5 million and free cash flow was negative $34.3 million.”

Outlook

  • “We anticipate that our full year net sales will be between $1.63 billion to $1.73 billion with a midpoint of $1.68 billion,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director), while “we are reiterating both our full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow guidance.”
  • “We expect our full year adjusted EBITDA to be between $275 million to $285 million and our full year cash -- free cash flow to be between $100 million and $120 million,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director).
  • “Adjusted gross margins for the year should be between 46% and 47%, and we expect these margins to improve sequentially throughout the year,” said Kraft (CFO & Treasurer).

Financial Results

  • “First quarter adjusted gross margin decreased by 130 basis points to 45.6%,” said Kraft (CFO & Treasurer), and “adjusted EBITDA to net sales margin during the quarter decreased by 170 basis points from a year ago to 13.5%.”
  • “We believe Q1 is the low watermark in our gross margin for the year,” said Kraft (CFO & Treasurer). He added, “as 2026 goes on, we expect to see margins improve as we work through high-cost tariff-impacted inventory.”
  • “We ended the first quarter of 2026 with $710 million of total net debt outstanding,” said Kraft (CFO & Treasurer), and “at quarter end, our net debt to trailing 12-month adjusted EBITDA ratio was 2.6x.”
  • “During the quarter, we deployed $10.1 million to buy back 1.2 million shares at an average price of $8.29 per share,” said Kraft (CFO & Treasurer), adding, “we plan to continue buying stock on a regular basis.”

Q&A

  • Lee Jagoda, CJS Securities, Inc.: “Where are those customers from like an inventory position standpoint?” Jon Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “we really saw destocking only in our PS business,” and “we feel like the worst of that is behind us.”
  • David Manthey, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: “talk about how you think about the trajectory of gross margin through 2026?” Robert Kraft (CFO & Treasurer): “we believe Q1 is the low watermark,” and “we see margins stepping up throughout the year.”
  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays Bank PLC: “is there anything kind of bigger picture going on from a structural perspective” in PS? Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “promotional activity was the biggest portion of that drop in Q1,” and “the core is healthy.”
  • Bouley, Barclays Bank PLC: “why the net impact would still be neutral” on tariffs? Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “when you take the tailwind from the IEEPA and then the headwind from 232 and it is nearly a wash in totality.”
  • W. Andrew Carter, Stifel: “the magnitude of kind of the differences between March, January, February?” Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “January and February were rough months,” and “we did see that continue in April,” while adding, “we’re not going to start breaking that out now.”
  • Reuben Garner, The Benchmark Company: “talk about the ways that you can… improve the profitability on the acquisitions” and whether RDS margins “bottoming”? Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “we believe we can take that business and make it a nice contributor… on bottom line,” and on RDS, “we think profitability will be steady over throughout the year.”
  • Brian McNamara, Canaccord Genuity: “Does having those 2 deals in the bag by mid-April make a third one more likely this year?” Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director): “I would say there’s a high probability we’ll see another this year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, pressing on PS weakness, tariff mechanics, gross margin cadence, and intra-quarter sales trends, including “how low is low” type framing on margins and requests for monthly detail.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing control points and uncertainty, including “we’re not going to start breaking that out now” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi) and “we believe Q1 is the low watermark” (CFO & Treasurer Kraft).
  • Versus last quarter: management maintained confidence but shifted from Q4’s “record” framing to Q1 normalization language, while analysts continued recurring themes on Protective Solutions volatility and tariff/margin timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 raised 2026 net sales guidance to “between $1.63 billion to $1.73 billion” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi), compared with Q4’s initial “between $1.6 billion and $1.7 billion” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi).
  • Q1 moved M&A from pipeline to execution with “we closed on 2 acquisitions” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi), while Q4 said “The M&A pipeline is healthy” and “we feel confident we’ll do 1 to 2 deals in 2026” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi).
  • Q1 emphasized Pro as a growth pillar with “Growing the Pro channel is a new critical initiative” and “expand our addressable market by $12 billion” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi), aligning with Q4’s buildup that Pro would “broaden our go-to-market channels” (President, CEO & Director Adinolfi).

Risks and Concerns

  • “Uncertainty consumers are feeling due to the current economic environment impacted our results,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director).
  • “The timing of how tariffs have impacted our bottom line have been and will continue to be choppy,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director), while adding, “the net impact to Hillman is neutral.”
  • “More recently, the price of oil has increased,” said Adinolfi (President, CEO & Director), and “if these amounts do become material, we will price for them as we’ve done in the past.”

Final Takeaway

Hillman’s management centered Q1 around a tariff-driven margin trough, a Protective Solutions slowdown tied to promotions and destocking, and improving momentum in Robotics and Digital Solutions via the MinuteKey 3.5 rollout. The company raised full-year net sales guidance by $30 million following the Campbell Chain & Fittings and Delaney Hardware acquisitions while reiterating adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow ranges, and it positioned Pro expansion and additional M&A as key drivers toward its $2.5 billion net sales target for 2030.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Galaxy targets Helios Phase I delivery of 133 megawatts by end of Q2 as it estimates $90M Q2-to-date adjusted EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Galaxy Digital (GLXY) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "First quarter crypto prices down on average of 25%, you can see our headline number, certainly, isn't what we'd like to be delivering quarter after quarter. All that said, I feel pretty good about the business right now." (Founder, CEO & Director Michael Novogratz)
  • "We delivered our first data halls. We're on schedule." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz)
  • "I was hoping we would have the definitive deal details today... that will come in a very short order, we will have Phase II financed." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz)
  • "We've got this 830 megawatts that we were granted recently tenant for that... My sense is that's probably going to be a second half of the year process." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz)
  • "For the first quarter, we reported a GAAP net loss of $216 million or a loss of $0.49 per share and firm-wide adjusted EBITDA of negative $188 million." (Chief Financial Officer Anthony Paquette)
  • "Revenue will begin ramping in Q2 as we deliver data halls under our CoreWeave lease agreement... 15-year contracted cash flows at approximately 90% average lease level EBITDA margins entirely uncorrelated to Digital Asset prices." (CFO Paquette)
  • "So far in Q2... second quarter-to-date adjusted EBITDA estimated at approximately $90 million through last Friday." (CFO Paquette)
  • "We've delivered the first data hall to CoreWeave... We remain on track to deliver substantially all of the 133 megawatts of critical IT capacity for Phase I by the end of Q2." (President & Chief Investment Officer Christopher Ferraro)

Outlook

  • Management did not provide full-quarter GAAP EPS or revenue guidance in this transcript; instead, it provided a Q2-to-date profitability indicator: "second quarter-to-date adjusted EBITDA estimated at approximately $90 million through last Friday." (CFO Paquette)
  • For Helios, management reiterated near-term delivery timing and the revenue ramp: "revenue will begin ramping in Q2" (CFO Paquette) and "deliver substantially all of the 133 megawatts... by the end of Q2" (President Ferraro).
  • Relative to the prior quarter’s public remarks, the emphasis shifted from timing an "announcement of a new tenant" (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz, Q4 AMA) to describing current-stage progress: "we are in active discussions with a select group of potential customers" for the "830 megawatts" and noted the process "requires extensive diligence, bespoke structuring and careful negotiation." (President Ferraro)

Financial results

  • "We reported a GAAP net loss of $216 million" and "firm-wide adjusted EBITDA of negative $188 million," which the company attributed "primarily" to "unrealized mark-to-market losses on our balance sheet." (CFO Paquette)
  • Galaxy reported cost actions alongside the downturn: "Firm-wide operating expenses... were approximately $147 million in Q1. Down 7% quarter-over-quarter." (CFO Paquette)
  • The company highlighted operating-business resilience: "our Digital Asset segment generated $49 million of adjusted gross profit, roughly in line with Q4 results despite broad market weakness in Q1." (CFO Paquette)
  • Capital return and liquidity were positioned as ongoing considerations: "we repurchased 3.2 million shares... for $65 million" and ended with "approximately $2.6 billion" of "cash and stablecoin." (CFO Paquette)

Q&A

  • Peter Christiansen, Citi: asked about data center financing conditions and syndication strain; President Ferraro said "the high-yield bond market has definitely stepped in" and "we've pretty much seen spreads tighten across the board."
  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: asked whether Helios 830 MW would be separate tenants vs. CoreWeave; Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz replied "on the 830, we're engaged in lots of conversations," and President Ferraro added "we are very focused on a multi-tenant strategy as well."
  • Goldman Sachs (speaking for James Yaro): asked about crypto trading clients’ risk appetite; Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz said "volumes across all of crypto trading was down... 25%... We felt good that we were flat" and tied an inflection to catalysts including "the CLARITY Act passing" and rates.
  • James Faucette, Morgan Stanley: pressed on ERCOT batch process risks; President Ferraro said on the 830 MW "we're equally confident" and that "the 1.8 gigawatts... is what's in question from a timing perspective."
  • Bill Papanastasiou, Chardan: asked about lease economics for uncontracted capacity; President Ferraro emphasized tenant credit and said the company focuses on "net after financing cost spread capture."
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: asked whether smaller-token demand is cyclical or secular post-CLARITY; Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz said his view "tend[s] towards" consolidation, adding "those tokens... are going to either have to restructure or they're going to slowly, slowly have less and less participation."
  • Benjamin Sommers, BTIG: asked about GalaxyOne usage; President Ferraro said "crypto trading has been the largest use case so far" and flagged a next step: "offering really financing solutions for individual consumer users."
  • Chris Brendler, Rosenblatt: asked about lending weakness; President Ferraro said "we saw a pullback" driven by price mechanics and roll-offs, emphasizing "the #1 KPI is that we don't lose money."
  • Jonathan Petersen, Jefferies: asked about using end-user guarantees to improve debt pricing and about splitting the data center business; President Ferraro said "all options are on the table" and on separation "no update," while Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz added it is "more of a debate... around the end of the year."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on financing conditions, ERCOT approval risk, lease economics, lending softness, and structural questions like separation.
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive around execution: "The lights are on at the Helios campus" and "single most important derisking event." (President Ferraro) In Q&A, management stayed neutral and procedural, repeatedly emphasizing process and uncertainty for incremental ERCOT capacity.
  • Versus the prior quarter’s AMA tone (more frustration with crypto prices and stock performance), management sounded more operationally confident on Helios delivery milestones while maintaining caution on timing for the 830 MW lease and for the 1.8 GW ERCOT study process.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The prior quarter framed crypto as "a bear market" with Bitcoin in a "$60,000, $80,000 range" (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz, Q4 AMA), while the current call described a "transition year" and highlighted early signs of decoupling: "prices were around 20%. Volumes... down 20%... and here at Galaxy, volumes were flat." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz)
  • In Q4, management emphasized the newly awarded "830 megawatts" and suggested tenant talks would be with "3 to 4" counterparties "within a short period of time." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz, Q4 AMA) In Q1, management shifted to milestone-based progress and deliberate leasing language: "extensive diligence" and "careful negotiation." (President Ferraro)
  • Q4 included a more explicit statement on perceived conglomerate discount: "Right now, I don't think we're getting value for having 2 businesses under one roof." (Founder, CEO & Director Novogratz, Q4 AMA) Q1 maintained optionality: "no update" and "we're not convinced that that's not true in the future." (President Ferraro)

Risks and concerns

  • "Q1 was a challenging quarter for Digital Asset prices" and results were "driven primarily by unrealized mark-to-market losses." (CFO Paquette)
  • Helios scaling risk centered on customer contracting and regulatory process: "A lease of this scale... requires extensive diligence" (President Ferraro) and "PGRR145 is still in draft form and could change." (President Ferraro)
  • Timing uncertainty persists for incremental interconnection beyond the approved 830 MW: "the 1.8 gigawatts... is what's in question from a timing perspective." (President Ferraro)

Final takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1’s reported loss reflected market-driven marks while portraying operating performance as steadier, pointing to flat trading volumes against an industry decline and $49 million in digital-asset adjusted gross profit. The company positioned Helios as the primary near-term catalyst, highlighting first data-hall delivery, an end-of-Q2 target to deliver substantially all 133 MW for Phase I, and a Q2 revenue ramp under a 15-year CoreWeave lease. Management also flagged near-term updates on Phase II financing, maintained a deliberate posture on leasing the 830 MW expansion, and said it is evaluating—but not updating—any plans to separate the data center business.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Twilio added as a new long idea at Hedgeye
data.description:
Twilio (TWLO) was added as a new long idea at Hedgey with the potential for 30% upside from current levels.

"Twilio is in the middle of an operational turnaround that is producing the cleanest financial profile in the company's history at exactly the moment its core voice product is being reshaped by AI," Hedgeye analyst Andrew Freedman wrote in a note on Tuesday. "Voice AI and high-margin software are the levers that move gross margins higher over time, and the combination of accelerating growth and improving underlying gross margin plus operating leverage is what drives the 12-month re-rate."

Hedgeye's Freedman added that "accelerating organic growth, improving ex-carrier-fee gross margin, narrowing dilution, and disciplined buyback together describe the textbook setup for multiple expansion on a software business that has been trading like a utility."

Twilio (TWLO) is scheduled to report Q1 results on Thursday.

data.title: OpenAI growth headwinds ripple through partners as related stocks fall
data.description:
Shares of OpenAI (OPENAI)-related companies, such as SoftBank Group (SFTBY) and Oracle (ORCL), fell on Tuesday after The Wall Street Journal reported the AI startup recently missed targets for sales and new users, fueling growth concerns. 

SoftBank tumbled as much as 11% in Tokyo, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) was down roughly 3%, CoreWeave (CRWV) fell 3.5%, Nvidia (NVDA) eased 1%, and Oracle (ORCL) lost 4.4%.

SoftBank, which owns approximately 11% of OpenAI, suffered its worst single-day percentage loss in six months.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman responded to the concerns and told CNBC that the report  was “ridiculous” and added that he and CFO Sarah Friar “are totally aligned on buying as much compute as we can.” 

The Journal reported that Friar has told other company leaders she is worried the company may not be able to pay for future computing contracts if revenue doesn’t grow fast enough.

While OpenAI has struck deals with dozens of firms, markets tend to focus on a smaller subset of major partners, including Nvidia (NVDA), SoftBank, Oracle, Microsoft (MSFT), Coreweave, and AMD, as investment proxies for the creator of ChatGPT.

Investors are closely watching for signs that tech companies remain committed to their previously announced plans for massive capital spending on building out AI infrastructure. “That’s what the market needs to see to keep the AI narrative intact,” Amanda Lyons, head of research at Energy Group Capital, told Bloomberg.

“The nuance is that it’s a narrow path: any hint of slowing spend would be taken negatively for the ecosystem, but a sharp step-up would likely raise questions around returns and sustainability.”

The Sam Altman-led firm fell short of several monthly sales targets in 2026 after rival Anthropic (ANTHRO) gained ground in the coding and enterprise markets, The Journal added.

(This story has been updated with additional information.)

data.title: IBM in spotlight as HSBC upgrades on margins, valuation
data.description:
• IBM (IBM) was in the spotlight as HSBC upgraded the tech giant to Hold from Neutral, citing improving margins and valuation.
  • “Non-GAAP operating margin came at 15.7%, +195bp y-o-y and c85bp above expectations,” analyst Stephen Bersey wrote in a note to clients, referencing IBM's most recent results. “Non-GAAP operating profit came at $2.505B, c7% above HSBCe and consensus. IBM has realized $4.5B of productivity savings since 2023 and expects another $1B in 2026e. The company maintained its previous 2026 guidance. It continues to expect 2026 constant currency revenue growth at more than 5% but emphasized software is likely to grow by more than 10% (previously by 10%), helped by earlier than expected closing of the Confluent acquisition. Non-GAAP PBT margin is likely to rise by about 1 pt y-o-y despite the margin dilutive Confluent acquisition.”
  • Bersey also noted that IBM's quantum computing business looks like it may offer first-mover advantage. “Management emphasized that the company remains on track to deliver the first, large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computer by 2029 and IBM’s partners will achieve the first examples of quantum advantage this year, leveraging IBM hardware.”

data.title: Pakistan said to receive new peace proposal from Iran soon - report
data.description:
Mediators in Pakistan anticipate receiving a new proposal from Iran within days aimed at ending the Middle East conflict, according to a media report on Tuesday.

The expected revision follows U.S. President Donald Trump’s rejection of an earlier Iranian framework. 

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Aragchi was scheduled to return to Tehran today after meetings in Russia, where CNN reported that he will consult regime leadership, citing sources familiar with the mediation process.

Those discussions are proceeding slowly because of challenges in reaching Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who remains in a secret location for security purposes, CNN reported.

Trump signaled over the weekend he would not accept the proposal Iran submitted, which sought to halt hostilities first while deferring more contentious negotiations over Tehran’s nuclear program.

Sources described the situation as fluid, the report said, with the outcome largely dependent on whether Iran presents a revised framework more palatable to Washington.

On Tuesday, Trump posted on social media that Iran has told the U.S. it is “in a ‘State of Collapse,’” claiming Tehran wants the Strait of Hormuz reopened while “they try to figure out their leadership.”

data.title: Embraer ends Q1 with record $32.1B backlog, up 22% Y/Y
data.description:
Embraer (EMBJ) down 1.2% in Tuesday's trading, despite disclosing its firm order ​backlog rose 22% Y/Y to a ⁠record $32.1B ​at the end of Q1, marking the Brazilian planemaker’s sixth consecutive quarterly record.

The biggest boost came from the commercial aviation unit, where backlog jumped 50% Y/Y to $15B that was largely tied to 18 firm orders for the Embraer E195-E2 from Finnair, part of a broader agreement covering up to 46 aircraft, including options and purchase rights.

Services and support continued to expand, with backlog rising 11% to a record-high $5.1B, as Embraer (EMBJ) signed long-term agreements with Airnorth, the Hungarian Air Force, and Virgin Australia.

Backlog in Embraer's (EMBJ) executive aircraft business stayed flat at $7.6B both Y/Y and Q/Q, even as deliveries increased to 29 aircraft from 23 a year earlier.

In defense, the company's backlog reached $4.4B, up 5% Y/Y but down 4% Q/Q.

The company said it delivered 44 aircraft across all business units in Q1, a 47% increase compared with 30 deliveries in the year-earlier quarter.
